name: Hydrogen
Analysis:
Electron Gain Enthalpy measures how much energy is released when an atom gains an electron. The more negative the value, the greater the atom's tendency to accept an electron.

Hydrogen is the standout in Group 1. Its tiny size allows the incoming electron to experience a very strong attraction from the nucleus, giving it the most negative electron gain enthalpy in the group.

From lithium (Li) to cesium (Cs), the electron gain enthalpy becomes less negative. As atomic size increases, the incoming electron enters a shell farther from the nucleus, where the attractive force is weaker and less energy is released.

But francium (Fr) adds a final twist. Despite being larger than cesium, its electron gain enthalpy is slightly more negative. This is due to relativistic effects, which contract and stabilize the 7s orbital, allowing francium to attract the incoming electron a little more strongly than expected.

name: Helium
Analysis:
Group 18 is the one group that simply doesn't want another electron! The noble gases have positive electron gain enthalpies, meaning that adding an electron requires energy rather than releasing it. Their completely filled valence shells are already exceptionally stable, so any extra electron must enter a higher-energy orbital.

Helium is the most extreme case. Its incoming electron must occupy the distant 2s orbital, far above the filled 1s shell, giving it an exceptionally large positive electron gain enthalpy.

As we move down the group, the energy gap to the next available orbital decreases, and the orbitals become larger and more diffuse. As a result, electron gain becomes less unfavorable. By the time we reach xenon, the electron gain enthalpy is only slightly positive, explaining why xenon can form stable compounds despite being a noble gas.

Reliable experimental values for neon, krypton, radon, and oganesson are unavailable. However, theoretical studies suggest that relativistic effects become increasingly important in the heavier noble gases, particularly radon and oganesson, influencing their electron-accepting behavior.

name: Beryllium
Analysis:
Group 2 elements don't like gaining electrons! Their filled ns² valence shell is highly stable, so adding an extra electron is generally unfavourable.

Beryllium and magnesium have positive electron gain enthalpies, strongly resisting electron gain because the incoming electron must enter a higher-energy np orbital.

As we move down the group, the energy gap between the ns and np orbitals decreases, making electron addition less unfavourable. As a result, the electron gain enthalpy becomes gradually more negative from calcium to barium.

Radium adds a final twist. Its value is slightly more negative than expected, most likely because relativistic effects modify its electronic structure, allowing it to accept an electron a little more readily than the lighter Group 2 elements.

name: Boron
Analysis:
Group 13 follows an irregular electron gain enthalpy trend, where several competing factors shape the ability to gain an electron. The values become more negative from boron (B) to aluminium (Al), remain almost unchanged for gallium (Ga), become less negative for indium (In), and then increase slightly again for thallium (Tl).

All Group 13 elements have the valence configuration ns²np¹. Gaining an electron forms ns²np², which offers no special half-filled or fully filled stability, so their electron gain enthalpies are only moderately negative.

Boron has the least negative value because the extra electron enters its compact 2p orbital, where electron–electron repulsion is high due to high electron density. Aluminium accepts an electron more readily because of larger 3p orbital. Gallium closely follows aluminium because the poor shielding by the 3d electrons increases the effective nuclear charge, compensating for its larger size.

Indium shows a less negative value as its larger atomic size weakens the attraction for the incoming electron. Thallium, however, breaks the trend. Lanthanide contraction, caused by the poor shielding of the 4f electrons, increases the effective nuclear charge, making electron gain slightly more favorable.

Nihonium remains largely theoretical. Although experimental data are unavailable, strong relativistic effects are expected to play a major role in determining its electron gain enthalpy.

name: Carbon
Analysis:
Group 14 shows a fascinating balance between stability and atomic size. The electron gain enthalpy is most negative for silicon (Si) and then becomes progressively less negative down the group, reaching a much smaller value for lead (Pb).

All Group 14 elements have the valence configuration ns²np². Gaining an electron forms the relatively stable ns²np³ configuration, a half-filled p subshell that is stabilized by exchange energy.

Silicon has a slightly more negative electron gain enthalpy than carbon because the incoming electron enters the larger and more diffuse 3p subshell, where the electrons are farther apart and experience lower mutual electron–electron repulsion than in carbon's compact 2p subshell.

From silicon to lead, the increasing atomic size gradually weakens the nucleus' attraction for the added electron, making electron gain less favorable. Although germanium experiences a higher effective nuclear charge due to the poor shielding of the 3d electrons, this is not enough to offset the effect of increasing atomic size. Lead shows the greatest drop because the inert pair effect stabilizes its 6s² electrons, reducing its tendency to accept an additional electron.

Flerovium remains largely theoretical. Although experimental data are unavailable, strong relativistic effects are predicted to modify its 7s and 7p orbitals, significantly influencing its electron gain enthalpy.

name: Nitrogen
Analysis:
Group 15 has one of the most unusual electron gain enthalpy trends. Nitrogen stands out with a positive electron gain enthalpy, while the values become increasingly negative from phosphorus (P) to antimony (Sb) before becoming slightly less negative for bismuth (Bi).

Why is nitrogen so different? Its stable half-filled 2p³ configuration and compact 2p orbitals make the addition of an electron highly unfavorable because it introduces strong electron–electron repulsion.

From phosphorus to antimony, the larger 3p, 4p, and 5p orbitals reduce the electron–electron pairing repulsion experienced by the incoming electron, making electron gain progressively more favorable. Antimony has the most negative electron gain enthalpy because its diffuse 5p orbitals comfortably accommodate the added electron.

Bismuth breaks the trend. The inert pair effect and relativistic stabilization of the 6s² electrons make the neutral atom unusually stable, so gaining an electron provides less additional stabilization. As a result, bismuth's electron gain enthalpy is less negative than antimony's.

Moscovium remains largely theoretical, with strong relativistic effects expected to play a major role in its electron gain behavior.

name: Oxygen
Analysis:
Group 16 elements readily gain an electron, but their trend has an interesting twist. The electron gain enthalpy is most negative for sulfur (S) and then becomes gradually less negative from selenium (Se) to polonium (Po).

All Group 16 elements have the valence configuration ns²np⁴. Gaining an electron forms the ns²np⁵ configuration, making electron gain energetically favorable.

Oxygen is the exception. Despite its small size, its compact 2p orbitals create strong electron–electron repulsion, making its electron gain enthalpy less negative than sulfur's. Sulfur's larger 3p orbitals reduce this repulsion, giving it the most negative electron gain enthalpy in the group.
From sulfur to polonium, increasing atomic size and shielding reduce the attraction between the nucleus and the incoming electron, making electron gain progressively less favorable.

Livermorium remains largely theoretical, with strong relativistic effects expected to significantly influence its electron gain enthalpy.

name: Fluorine
Analysis:
Group 17 contains the champions of electron gain. All the halogens have highly negative electron gain enthalpies because gaining one electron completes their stable noble-gas configuration (ns²np⁶).

But the biggest surprise is that chlorine—not fluorine—has the most negative electron gain enthalpy. Although fluorine is the most electronegative element, its tiny 2p orbitals are so compact that the incoming electron experiences strong electron–electron repulsion. In chlorine, the larger 3p orbitals reduce this repulsion while still allowing strong nuclear attraction, making electron gain even more favorable.

From chlorine to astatine, the electron gain enthalpy becomes progressively less negative as increasing atomic size and shielding weaken the attraction for the incoming electron. In astatine, relativistic effects further influence the trend, although the larger atomic size remains the dominant factor.

Tennessine remains largely theoretical. Its electron gain enthalpy has not been measured, but strong relativistic effects are predicted to alter its valence orbitals and reduce its tendency to gain an electron compared with the lighter halogens.

name: Scandium
Analysis:
Group 3 follows an unusual trend in electron gain enthalpy. Instead of becoming less negative down the group, the values become more negative from scandium (Sc) to lanthanum (La).

Why? The incoming electron enters an empty d orbital, creating a more stable d² configuration with greater exchange energy as compared to d1 configuration. In yttrium and lanthanum, the larger, more diffuse 4d and 5d orbitals can also accommodate the extra electron more easily.

These stabilizing effects outweigh the increase in atomic size, making electron gain more favourable down the group.

Actinium shows a slight deviation from the trend due to the combined influence of relativistic effects and the onset of actinide behavior.

name: Titanium
Analysis: The electron gain enthalpy of Group 4 elements shows an irregular trend. Titanium (−8 kJ mol⁻¹), zirconium (−41 kJ mol⁻¹), and hafnium (−1.3 kJ mol⁻¹) all gain an electron to form a d3 configuration, which is more stable than d2 because it possesses greater exchange energy. This stabilization contributes to the more negative electron gain enthalpy of zirconium. Although hafnium has nearly the same atomic radius as zirconium due to lanthanide contraction, the increased effective nuclear charge arising from the poor shielding by the 4f electrons is not sufficient to produce a more favorable electron gain. The unusually small electron gain enthalpy of hafnium reflects the subtle balance of effective nuclear charge, exchange energy, orbital energies, and electron correlation effects rather than atomic size alone. Reliable experimental data for rutherfordium are unavailable because of its extremely short half-life, although theoretical studies suggest that strong relativistic effects significantly alter the energies of the 6d and 7s orbitals, reducing its tendency to gain an electron. Thus, the trend is governed by a complex interplay of exchange energy, effective nuclear charge, orbital energies, electron correlation, and relativistic effects.

name: Vanadium
Analysis:
Group 5 displays one of the most dramatic electron gain enthalpy trends among the transition metals! The values become more negative from vanadium (V) to niobium (Nb), then less negative for tantalum (Ta), and are predicted to be close to zero for dubnium (Db).

Niobium steals the spotlight. By gaining an electron, it achieves the exceptionally stable half-filled 4d⁵ configuration, maximizing exchange energy and making electron gain highly favorable. This gives niobium the most negative electron gain enthalpy in the group.

Tantalum breaks the trend. The added electron forms a 5d⁴ configuration, which is less stable than a half-filled d⁵ subshell.

Dubnium remains a mystery. Although experimental data are unavailable, theoretical studies suggest that strong relativistic effects alter the 6d and 7s orbitals, reducing its tendency to gain an electron.

name: Chromium
Analysis:
Group 6 shows a remarkably smooth increase in electron affinity! The electron gain enthalpy becomes more negative from chromium (Cr) to molybdenum (Mo) and tungsten (W), indicating a growing tendency to accept an electron.

All three elements have a d⁵s¹ valence configuration. On gaining an electron, they form the particularly stable d⁵s² configuration, preserving the half-filled d⁵ subshell while completing the s orbital.

As we move from chromium to tungsten, the larger 4d and 5d orbitals accommodate the incoming electron more easily. In tungsten, lanthanide contraction further strengthens the effective nuclear charge, stabilizing the added electron and giving tungsten the most negative electron gain enthalpy in the group.

Seaborgium remains largely unexplored. Although experimental data are unavailable, theoretical studies suggest that strong relativistic effects modify the 6d and 7s orbitals, making electron gain less favorable than expected.

name: Manganese
Analysis:
Group 7 presents one of the most irregular electron gain enthalpy trends among the transition metals. The values change from about 0 kJ mol⁻¹ for manganese (Mn) to −53 kJ mol⁻¹ for technetium (Tc), become less negative for rhenium (Re), and are predicted to be close to zero for bohrium (Bh).

Manganese begins with an exceptionally stable half-filled 3d⁵ configuration. Adding an electron disrupts this stability, so electron gain is neither strongly favorable nor unfavorable.

Technetium breaks the trend. Its larger, more diffuse 4d orbitals can accommodate the incoming electron more easily, making electron gain much more favorable despite the loss of the half-filled configuration.

Rhenium reverses the trend. Although it also has a half-filled 5d5 configuration, electron gain forms a 5d6 configuration with lower exchange energy. The electron gain enthalpy is determined by the competing effects of exchange energy, effective nuclear charge, lanthanide contraction, and relativistic effects, resulting in a less negative value than technetium.

Bohrium remains largely theoretical. Its electron gain enthalpy has not been measured, but strong relativistic effects are predicted to alter the 6d and 7s orbitals, making electron gain less favorable.

name: Iron
Analysis:
Group 8 shows a clear increase in the tendency to gain an electron. From iron (Fe) to ruthenium (Ru) and osmium (Os), the electron gain enthalpy becomes increasingly negative, indicating that electron gain becomes more favorable.

Why? The larger, more diffuse 4d and 5d orbitals of ruthenium and osmium accommodate the incoming electron more easily than iron's compact 3d orbitals, reducing electron–electron repulsion.

Osmium has the most negative electron gain enthalpy in the group. Lanthanide contraction increases the effective nuclear charge through the poor shielding of the 4f electrons, while relativistic effects further stabilize the added electron in the 5d orbitals.

Hassium remains largely theoretical. Although experimental data are unavailable, calculations suggest that strong relativistic effects significantly modify the 6d and 7s orbitals, making electron gain less favorable than expected.

name: Cobalt
Analysis:
Group 9 shows a striking increase in the tendency to gain an electron. The electron gain enthalpy becomes progressively more negative from cobalt (Co) to rhodium (Rh) and reaches its most negative value in iridium (Ir).

Cobalt gains an electron only moderately easily because the incoming electron enters the compact 3d orbital, where electron–electron repulsion is relatively high.

In rhodium and especially iridium, the extra electron enters the 4d and 5d orbitals, where it is stabilized more effectively. In iridium, lanthanide contraction and the poor shielding by the 4f electrons increase the effective nuclear charge, while relativistic effects further strengthen the attraction for the added electron.

As a result, the increasing nuclear attraction outweighs the accompanying electron–electron repulsion, making electron gain progressively more favorable from Co → Rh → Ir.

Meitnerium remains largely unexplored. Although experimental data are unavailable, theoretical studies suggest that strong relativistic effects significantly alter the 6d and 7s orbitals, reducing its tendency to gain an electron.

name: Nickel
Analysis:
Group 10 displays one of the most dramatic electron gain enthalpy trends in the periodic table! The values change from nickel (Ni) to palladium (Pd), become highly negative for platinum (Pt), and are predicted to be close to zero for darmstadtium (Ds).

Nickel readily gains an electron to form a 3d⁹ configuration, giving it a moderately negative electron gain enthalpy.

Palladium breaks the trend. With its exceptionally stable 4d¹⁰ configuration, the incoming electron must enter a higher-energy orbital, making electron gain much less favorable. As a result, palladium has a much less negative electron gain enthalpy than nickel.

Then comes platinum—the star of the group. Gaining an electron produces the highly stable 5d¹⁰6s¹ configuration. Lanthanide contraction, together with the poor shielding of the 4f electrons and relativistic effects, strongly stabilizes the 5d orbitals, giving platinum the most negative electron gain enthalpy in the group.

Darmstadtium remains largely theoretical. Although experimental data are unavailable, strong relativistic effects are predicted to alter the 6d and 7s orbitals, making electron gain much less favorable than in platinum.

name: Copper
Analysis:
Group 11 shows a remarkable increase in the tendency to gain an electron. The electron gain enthalpy becomes more negative from copper (Cu) to silver (Ag) and reaches an exceptionally large negative value for gold (Au) before being predicted to be close to zero for roentgenium (Rg).

Copper, silver, and gold all have the valence configuration (n−1)d¹⁰ns¹. On gaining an electron, they form the highly stable (n−1)d¹⁰ns² configuration, completing the outermost s orbital while retaining the stable filled d¹⁰ subshell.

Gold is the standout. Lanthanide contraction, caused by the poor shielding of the 4f electrons, increases the effective nuclear charge, while strong relativistic effects further stabilize the 6s orbital. Together, these effects make the added electron exceptionally stable, giving gold the most negative electron gain enthalpy in the group.

Roentgenium remains largely theoretical. Although experimental data are unavailable, strong relativistic effects are predicted to alter the 6d and 7s orbitals, reducing its tendency to gain an electron despite its enormous nuclear charge.

name: Zinc
Analysis:
Group 12 has one of the simplest electron gain enthalpy trends—these elements simply don't want another electron! Zinc (Zn), cadmium (Cd), and mercury (Hg) all have electron gain enthalpies as zero, indicating almost no tendency to accept an extra electron.

Why? Each already possesses the exceptionally stable (n−1)d¹⁰ns² configuration, with both a filled d subshell and a filled outermost s subshell. Any additional electron would have to enter a higher-energy p orbital, making electron gain energetically unfavorable.

Mercury is even more reluctant. Strong relativistic effects stabilize its 6s electrons, making the 5d¹⁰6s² configuration even more stable and further reducing its tendency to gain an electron.

Copernicium is expected to behave similarly. Although experimental data are unavailable, even stronger relativistic effects are predicted to reinforce its stable electronic configuration, making electron gain highly unfavorable.
